dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have chest pain on the right side, feels like pressure. It started around 1 PM and I rate it as an 8 out of 10.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is George Smith, and I believe I'm in my house in Richmond.
Can you move your fingers and toes on command?
Your airway is patent and clear. Any difficulty with speaking or breathing?
You're breathing a bit faster, and I notice you're using pursed-lip breathing. Were you experiencing shortness of breath before calling 911?
Your pulse is strong and regular. Skin is warm with normal tone and no signs of bleeding.
Mr. Smith, are there any other symptoms besides the chest pain?
No other symptoms. I had pneumonia in February, but otherwise I feel okay.
Do you have any known allergies?
No known drug or food allergies.
What medications are you currently taking?
Lisinopril, Atorvastatin, Albuterol, Naproxen, and a few more. I can list all of them if you'd like.
How severe is the pain now on a scale from 0 to 10?
It's still an 8, like a constant pressure in the center of my right chest.
Does the pain get worse with deep breaths or movement?
Yes, definitely worse when I take deep breaths.
Pulse is 78 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations are 14 per minute. Breathing seems a bit increased.
Blood pressure is 109 over 75 mmHg.
SpO2 is 96%. Oxygen saturation is good.
Your blood glucose is currently 202 mg/dL.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm at this time.
We are going to start 4-lead ECG monitoring now at 17:18.
4-lead ECG monitoring has begun.
Mr. Smith, would you mind if I check for tenderness in your chest?
Go ahead.
Chest rise and fall are equal. Right upper chest is tender to palpation-no deformities or bruising noted.
Your pupils are equal and reactive. No signs of droop or asymmetry.
A 12-lead EKG was completed at 17:32 and showed possible infarct with abnormal Q waves.
Mr. Smith, you've mentioned COPD, CHF, diabetes, and hypertension. Any history of cardiac issues?
No heart attacks or surgeries, but I was diagnosed with COPD a couple of years ago.
As part of protocol, we need to start an IV in case we require medications or fluids.
IV started at 17:33 for potential medication administration.
Let me confirm your lung fields with auscultation. Any unusual breathing sounds or pain when I listen?
No wheezing. Just the chest discomfort on deep breaths.
Breath sounds are clear and equal. No adventitious sounds noted.
Pulse is 72 beats per minute and regular now.
Respirations are at 15 per minute.
Blood pressure is 120 over 69 mmHg.
SpO2 is 95% now.
Blood sugar is currently reading 0-likely due to the monitor not detecting. We'll monitor it.
Mr. Smith, we'll be transporting you to Memorial Regional Medical Center. That's okay with you?
Mr. Smith, we'll now move you from the bed to the stair chair and then onto the stretcher. Please remain still.
Pulse is 75 beats per minute.
Respirations are 21 per minute-breathing has increased slightly.
Blood pressure is now 112 over 59 mmHg.
SpO2 remains at 95%.
A second 12-lead EKG was performed at 18:15 and showed a first-degree heart block and possible Mobitz type II.
Mr. Smith, we'll be placing you on the stretcher in semi-Fowler's position with a five-point harness. Does this help your breathing?
Yes, this feels more comfortable.
We will monitor your vitals continuously during transport.
Mr. Smith, is the pain still at an 8 on the 0 to 10 scale?
No change-still an 8.
Any new or worsening symptoms since we arrived?
No new symptoms. Just the pressure in the right chest.
The hospital staff have been alerted and are preparing for your arrival.
Based on the EKG findings, vitals, and patient history, we are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac involvement.
Transport destination remains Memorial Regional Medical Center for triage.
Preparing the handoff report for the receiving hospital.
All necessary equipment and documentation are prepared for transfer.
Final check complete. Ready for patient handoff.
Hospital personnel are ready. Let's go over the patient's condition.

Mr. George Smith, 77-year-old male, presents with non-traumatic chest pain, 8/10 on the pain scale, mid-right in location, non-radiating. He has a history of COPD, CHF, non-insulin dependent diabetes, and hypertension. Initial glucose was 202 mg/dL, later undetectable. A 12-lead EKG suggests infarct and first-degree heart block. Aspirin 324mg administered, IV established. Ready for transfer.
